requirements.test_condition: The model must be able to represent graded levels of certainty/uncertainty for any assertion, not merely binary certain/uncertain.
The model must be able to represent multiple competing interpretations of the same evidence, each with its own certainty level, without forcing resolution.
The model must be able to explicitly represent that a piece of information is known to be missing (known unknown), distinguishing it from information that was never expected to exist.
Every assertion must be linkable to its source(s) with full provenance chain.
The model must be able to represent the epistemic distance between a claim and its evidentiary basis (e.g., eyewitness vs. hearsay vs. later reconstruction).
The model must allow multiple agents to hold conflicting assertions about the same referent simultaneously without requiring resolution.
The model must be able to represent relationships between perspectives (agreement, contradiction, subsumption, independence).
Each interpretation act must carry metadata: who interpreted, when, under which theoretical framework, and with what purpose.
The model must be able to represent that categorizations change over time and that a category valid in one period may be anachronistic in another.
The model must distinguish between categories used by historical actors (emic) and categories imposed by researchers (etic), and track the provenance of each.
The model must represent source type (textual, visual, material, oral, etc.) as an explicit dimension that affects the interpretation of content.
The model must be able to represent the original purpose for which a source was created, distinguishing it from its later use as historical evidence.

The model must be able to represent that certain categories of information are systematically absent from a source type due to its creation purpose (e.g., women absent from guild records not by accident but by design).
The model must be able to represent gaps in the transmission record — periods or regions for which sources are known to have existed but are now lost.
The model must separate the physical carrier (manuscript, codex, stone) from the informational content it carries, allowing independent description of each.
The model must be able to represent material properties of the carrier (ink color, watermarks, paper quality, damage) as informationally significant, not merely descriptive metadata.
The model must distinguish multiple temporal layers: time of the described event, time of the source creation, time of later annotations/copies, and time of scholarly interpretation.
The model must be able to represent historical reference systems (calendars, currencies, measurements) alongside modern equivalents without privileging either.
The model must be able to represent both diplomatic (source-faithful) and normalized readings of the same text, maintaining the relationship between them.
The model must be able to represent typed relationships between sources (copy, translation, commentary, citation, contradiction).
The model must separate source representation from interpretive assertions. The artifact representation must be as free from interpretation as possible.
The model should center on events as the primary unit of historical description, linking persons, places, times, and sources through events.
Adding new information or interpretations must not require deleting or modifying existing assertions. The model must be additive/monotonic.
All information in the model must be wrapped in assertions that attribute the information to an agent, a source, and a confidence level.

causes_of_poor_absence_modeling: Positive bias of knowledge representation: ontologies describe what exists, not what is missing
Open World Assumption (OWA) in OWL: missing statements = unknown, not = informationally significant
systemic_gap_analysis.owa_paradox: Historical research needs a knowledge representation that operates under OWA (because historical knowledge is always incomplete) and simultaneously can model absence as an informational state of affairs.
